question_title: What will be the value of "90-Day Average SOFR" on 2026-04-09?
summary: The 90-Day Average SOFR on 2026-04-09 is a mechanically-determined rolling compound average. From Mar 27 (current value 3.68416) to Apr 9, the window shifts: elevated late-Dec/early-Jan rates (including the Dec 31 year-end spike of 3.87% and Jan 2 post-holiday rate of 3.75%) drop out and are replaced by current SOFR rates (~3.62-3.65%). With no FOMC meeting before April 9 and the Fed funds rate stable at 3.50-3.75%, the base case projects a decline to approximately 3.671%. The distribution is rightfully tight because the 90-day rolling average divides each day's SOFR impact by 90, massively damping daily variation. Tail risk comes from geopolitical stress (US-Iran conflict) potentially disrupting repo markets or triggering an emergency rate hike, but even extreme scenarios barely move the 90-day average.

factors.description: Current 90-day avg SOFR is 3.68416 (Mar 27), with steady declining trend as elevated Dec/early-Jan rates leave the 90-day window and lower current rates (~3.63-3.65) enter. Deterministic projection with SOFR=3.64 yields ~3.671.
The Dec 31 year-end SOFR spike (3.87) and Jan 1 holiday carry (also 3.87) drop out of the window around Apr 1-2, causing a notable step-down in the average (~0.005 decline over those 2 days)
No FOMC meeting before April 9 (next is April 29). Fed funds rate stable at 3.50-3.75%. Only ~4% probability of rate action at April meeting per CME FedWatch.

Geopolitical tension (US-Iran conflict, Strait of Hormuz closure, oil >$110). Some traders hedging emergency rate hike (12% prob of 25bp at April FOMC). Could cause repo market stress pushing SOFR temporarily higher.

Quarter-end (Mar 31) repo market dynamics could briefly spike daily SOFR, but the 90-day average smooths this out (each day has only 1/90th weight). Even a 50bp spike for 2 days only moves the avg by ~0.01.

reasoning:
**Forecast Summary: 90-Day Average SOFR on 2026-04-09**

**Median estimate: 3.671%** (90% CI: 3.668% – 3.682%)

Key reasoning:
- The 90-day average is a mechanically-determined rolling compound average with very high inertia (each day = 1/90th weight)
- From Mar 27 to Apr 9, elevated late-Dec/early-Jan rates (including the 3.87% year-end spike) drop out and are replaced by current SOFR rates (~3.63-3.65%)
- No FOMC meeting before resolution; Fed funds rate stable at 3.50-3.75%
- The distribution is appropriately tight — even extreme repo market stress or an emergency rate hike would move the 90-day average by only ~0.01-0.03
- Right-tail risk comes from the US-Iran conflict potentially disrupting repo markets, but impact on a 90-day rolling average is inherently limited
resolution_criteria: Resolves to the value found on the FRED API for the series SOFR90DAYAVG once the data is published.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will paginate through the data found at the API endpoint `https://api.stlouisfed.org/fred/series/observations?series_id=SOFR90DAYAVG`. This endpoint includes the latest data for the series. The latest revised data will be used when the resolution is assessed. The datapoint matching 2026-04-09 will be used to determine the resolution of this question.

A datapoint matches if:
1. The series is updated daily and the date of the datapoint is within 1 day previous to the resolution date.
2. The series is updated weekly and the date of the datapoint is within 7 days previous to the resolution date.
3. The series is updated monthly and the date of the datapoint is within 31 days previous to the resolution date.

If the datapoint is clearly skipped, or no datapoint is found after 2 weeks of checking then the question will be annulled.
